title: 2023 DPA Gender Pay Gap Analysis
summary: Women earn 42% less than men overall in Singapore's design industry. The pay gap varies across job levels, correlating with responsibility. At the IC track, women earn 11-21% less than men across most levels, except at Junior IC where women earn 11% more. Men have nearly double the average years of design experience (7.47 vs 3.86 years for women). Men are more likely to negotiate (61% vs 51%), feel more fairly compensated, and report higher market rate awareness. Men report higher formal job levelling progress (62% vs 52%). Women lead both the highest (>20%) and no-pay-increase brackets. Women are slightly more likely to actively seek new jobs. Published per-level medians for Singapore respondents in SGD: Junior IC, Man S$47,600 (n=10) versus Woman S$54,000 (n=53), a gap of 11.85% with men earning less; Mid-level IC, Man S$83,900 (n=20) versus Woman S$74,400 (n=35), a gap of 11.32% with women earning less; Senior IC, Man S$133,150 (n=20) versus Woman S$105,050 (n=32), a gap of 21.1% with women earning less; People Manager, Man S$151,380 (n=13) versus Woman S$119,250 (n=10), a gap of 21.22% with women earning less. Lead/Staff/Principal IC and Director levels are not published at this granularity because at least one gender cohort in each falls below n=10 (Lead/Staff woman n=7; Director man n=6, woman n=1). Reported pay-rise distribution: no increase in the past year for 19.5% of men versus 20.6% of women; more than 20% increase for 24.4% of men versus 25.2% of women. Median years of design experience for the Singapore cohort overall was 7 for men versus 3 for women.
methodology: Analysis of gender-based compensation differences among Singapore respondents. Total compensation comparisons include base salary, bonuses, stock, and other compensation. Controlled for level of seniority, years of design experience, negotiation behaviour, and career progression indicators. Analysis limited to Singapore due to sample size constraints in other markets.
